question: Show countries where a singer above age 40 and a singer below 30 are from.

contract_validated.intent: Find countries that have at least one singer above age 40 and at least one singer below age 30.
notes: The planner's conditions 'Age > 40' AND 'Age < 30' are logically impossible on the same row; must check existence across different rows.
Use GROUP BY Country and HAVING to ensure both age conditions are met by at least one singer per country.
Use conditional aggregation in HAVING to count singers meeting each age criterion.
DISTINCT is needed because multiple singers per country may satisfy the conditions.
No joins needed — all data is in the singer table.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT DISTINCT singer.Country
FROM singer
GROUP BY singer.Country
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN singer.Age > 40 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
   AND SUM(CASE WHEN singer.Age < 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0;
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT DISTINCT s1.Country
FROM singer s1
JOIN singer s2 ON s1.Country = s2.Country
WHERE s1.Age > 40 AND s2.Age < 30;

final_sql:
SELECT DISTINCT singer.Country
FROM singer
GROUP BY singer.Country
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN singer.Age > 40 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
   AND SUM(CASE WHEN singer.Age < 30 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0;

singer_in_concert JOIN singer ON singer_in_concert.Singer_ID = singer.Singer_ID

notes: COUNT(singer_in_concert.Singer_ID) counts singers per concert; no DISTINCT needed.

SELECT

    COUNT(sc.Singer_ID) AS number_of_singers

FROM concert

question: Find the name and location of the stadiums which some concerts happened in the years of both 2014 and 2015.

joins: stadium INNER JOIN concert ON stadium.Stadium_ID = concert.Stadium_ID

contract_validated.intent: Find the name and location of stadiums that hosted concerts in both 2014 and 2015.
notes: The planner incorrectly uses two ANDed Year conditions which can never be true; must use aggregation to find stadiums with concerts in both years.
Use HAVING with conditional aggregation to ensure concerts occurred in both 2014 and 2015.
Stadium_ID is TEXT in concert table but NUMBER in stadium; join is still valid as per schema FK link.
No need for singer or singer_in_concert tables — question is about stadiums and years only.
Group by stadium attributes to aggregate concert years per stadium.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT stadium.Name, stadium.Location
FROM stadium
INNER JOIN concert ON stadium.Stadium_ID = concert.Stadium_ID
GROUP BY stadium.Stadium_ID, stadium.Name, stadium.Location
HAVING SUM(CASE WHEN concert.Year = '2014' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0
   AND SUM(CASE WHEN concert.Year = '2015' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) > 0;
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT DISTINCT s.Name, s.Location
FROM stadium s
JOIN concert c ON s.Stadium_ID = c.Stadium_ID
WHERE c.Year = '2014'
AND EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM concert c2
    WHERE c2.Stadium_ID = s.Stadium_ID
    AND c2.Year = '2015'
);

final_sql:
SELECT DISTINCT s.Name, s.Location
FROM stadium s
JOIN concert c ON s.Stadium_ID = c.Stadium_ID
WHERE c.Year = '2014'
AND EXISTS (
    SELECT 1
    FROM concert c2
    WHERE c2.Stadium_ID = s.Stadium_ID
    AND c2.Year = '2015'
);

question: Find the average ranking for each player and their first name.

FROM

FROM

question: Show names, results and bulgarian commanders of the battles with no ships lost in the 'English Channel'.

contract_validated.intent: Find battles in the English Channel with no ships lost, returning battle name, result, and Bulgarian commander.
notes: Planner incorrectly uses ship.lost_in_battle IS NULL to mean 'no ships lost' — this is wrong; NULL means no record, not 'no loss'.
Condition ship.location = 'English Channel' must be applied to ships that participated, not to filter battles via NULL.
To find battles with no ships lost in English Channel, exclude battles where any ship in English Channel was lost.
The join to ship is unnecessary in FROM; use subquery to filter battles with no losses in English Channel.

Schema shows ship.lost_in_battle references battle.id — so if a ship is lost in a battle, that field is the battle ID; if no ship was lost, no row exists for that battle in ship with location='English Channel'.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT battle.name, battle.result, battle.bulgarian_commander
FROM battle
WHERE battle.id NOT IN (
    SELECT ship.lost_in_battle
    FROM ship
    WHERE ship.location = 'English Channel'
    AND ship.lost_in_battle IS NOT NULL
);
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT b.name, b.result, b.bulgarian_commander
FROM battle b
JOIN ship s ON b.id = s.lost_in_battle
WHERE b.name = 'English Channel' AND s.lost_in_battle IS NULL;

final_sql:
SELECT battle.name, battle.result, battle.bulgarian_commander
FROM battle
WHERE battle.id NOT IN (
    SELECT ship.lost_in_battle
    FROM ship
    WHERE ship.location = 'English Channel'
    AND ship.lost_in_battle IS NOT NULL
);

question: Which department offers the most number of degrees? List department name and id.

contract_validated.intent: Find the department with the most degree programs, returning its name and ID.
notes: Join Departments and Degree_Programs via department_id FK.
Count degree programs per department to find the maximum.
Group by department_id and department_name to aggregate correctly.
Order by count descending and limit to 1 to get the top department.
No filtering conditions needed; all departments are eligible.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT Departments.department_name, Departments.department_id
FROM Departments
INNER JOIN Degree_Programs ON Departments.department_id = Degree_Programs.department_id
GROUP BY Departments.department_id, Departments.department_name
ORDER BY COUNT(Degree_Programs.degree_program_id) DESC
LIMIT 1;
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT d.department_name, d.department_id
FROM Departments d
JOIN Degree_Programs dp ON d.department_id = dp.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name
ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC
LIMIT 1;

SELECT Departments.department_name, Departments.department_id
FROM Departments
INNER JOIN Degree_Programs ON Departments.department_id = Degree_Programs.department_id
GROUP BY Departments.department_id, Departments.department_name
ORDER BY COUNT(Degree_Programs.degree_program_id) DESC
LIMIT 1;

question: What is the semester which most student registered in? Show both the name and the id.

question: For each semester, what is the name and id of the one with the most students registered?

contract_validated.intent: For each semester, find the name and ID of the semester with the most student enrollments.
notes: Count enrollments per semester using Student_Enrolment.student_id.
Group by semester_id and semester_name to aggregate per semester.
Order by count descending and limit to 1 to get the semester with most students.
Students table is not needed since student names are not requested; only semester name and ID.
Join is correctly based on FK: Student_Enrolment.semester_id → Semesters.semester_id.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT Semesters.semester_name, Semesters.semester_id
FROM Semesters
INNER JOIN Student_Enrolment ON Student_Enrolment.semester_id = Semesters.semester_id
GROUP BY Semesters.semester_id, Semesters.semester_name
ORDER BY COUNT(Student_Enrolment.student_id) DESC
LIMIT 1;
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT 
    s.semester_name,
    st.first_name,
    st.last_name,
    st.student_id
FROM Semesters s
JOIN Student_Enrolment se ON s.semester_id = se.semester_id
JOIN Students st ON se.student_id = st.student_id
GROUP BY s.semester_id, st.student_id
ORDER BY s.semester_id, COUNT(*) DESC
LIMIT 1;

final_sql:
SELECT Semesters.semester_name, Semesters.semester_id
FROM Semesters
INNER JOIN Student_Enrolment ON Student_Enrolment.semester_id = Semesters.semester_id
GROUP BY Semesters.semester_id, Semesters.semester_name
ORDER BY COUNT(Student_Enrolment.student_id) DESC
LIMIT 1;

question: Who are enrolled in 2 degree programs in one semester? List the first name, middle name and last name and the id.

joins: Students JOIN Student_Enrolment ON Students.student_id = Student_Enrolment.student_id
Student_Enrolment JOIN Degree_Programs ON Student_Enrolment.degree_program_id = Degree_Programs.degree_program_id
Student_Enrolment JOIN Semesters ON Student_Enrolment.semester_id = Semesters.semester_id
aggregations: GROUP BY Students.student_id, Students.first_name, Students.middle_name, Students.last_name

contract_validated.intent: Find students enrolled in exactly two degree programs in one semester and return their first name, middle name, last name, and student ID.
notes: Only need Degree_Programs to count enrolments; Semesters not needed since no filter on semester.
Join path is valid: Students -> Student_Enrolment -> Degree_Programs via FKs.
GROUP BY includes all non-aggregate SELECT columns as required.
HAVING clause correctly filters for exactly two degree programs per student.
No DISTINCT needed since grouping by student ensures uniqueness.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT Students.first_name, Students.middle_name, Students.last_name, Students.student_id
FROM Students
JOIN Student_Enrolment ON Students.student_id = Student_Enrolment.student_id
JOIN Degree_Programs ON Student_Enrolment.degree_program_id = Degree_Programs.degree_program_id
GROUP BY Students.student_id, Students.first_name, Students.middle_name, Students.last_name
HAVING COUNT(Degree_Programs.degree_program_id) = 2;
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT 
    s.first_name, 
    s.middle_name, 
    s.last_name, 
    s.student_id
FROM 
    Students s
JOIN 
    Student_Enrolment se ON s.student_id = se.student_id
GROUP BY 
    s.student_id, s.first_name, s.middle_name, s.last_name
HAVING 
    COUNT(se.degree_program_id) = 2;

final_sql:
SELECT Students.first_name, Students.middle_name, Students.last_name, Students.student_id
FROM Students
JOIN Student_Enrolment ON Students.student_id = Student_Enrolment.student_id
JOIN Degree_Programs ON Student_Enrolment.degree_program_id = Degree_Programs.degree_program_id
GROUP BY Students.student_id, Students.first_name, Students.middle_name, Students.last_name
HAVING COUNT(Degree_Programs.degree_program_id) = 2;

JOIN Transcript_Contents tc ON t.transcript_id = tc.transcript_id
JOIN Student_Enrolment_Courses sec ON tc.student_course_id = sec.student_course_id
GROUP BY t.transcript_id
HAVING COUNT(sec.course_id) >= 2
ORDER BY t.transcript_date, t.transcript_id
LIMIT 1;

final_sql:
SELECT Transcripts.transcript_id, Transcripts.transcript_date
FROM Transcripts
INNER JOIN Transcript_Contents ON Transcripts.transcript_id = Transcript_Contents.transcript_id
INNER JOIN Student_Enrolment_Courses ON Transcript_Contents.student_course_id = Student_Enrolment_Courses.student_course_id
GROUP BY Transcripts.transcript_id, Transcripts.transcript_date
HAVING COUNT(Student_Enrolment_Courses.course_id) >= 2;

question: Find the semester when both Master students and Bachelor students got enrolled in.

joins: Student_Enrolment JOIN Degree_Programs ON Student_Enrolment.degree_program_id = Degree_Programs.degree_program_id
Student_Enrolment JOIN Semesters ON Student_Enrolment.semester_id = Semesters.semester_id

contract_validated.intent: Find semesters where both Master and Bachelor students were enrolled, using the same semester_id.
notes: Original plan incorrectly uses AND between 'Master' and 'Bachelor' conditions — impossible in single row.
Must find semesters with BOTH types of students — use subqueries with IN.
Only Semesters table needed in main query; joins can be in subqueries.
degree_summary_name values are exact strings: 'Master' and 'Bachelor' (case-sensitive).
No aggregation or ordering required; question asks for semesters (names) only.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT Semesters.semester_name
FROM Semesters
WHERE Semesters.semester_id IN (
    SELECT se1.semester_id
    FROM Student_Enrolment se1
    JOIN Degree_Programs dp1 ON se1.degree_program_id = dp1.degree_program_id
    WHERE dp1.degree_summary_name = 'Master'
)
AND Semesters.semester_id IN (
    SELECT se2.semester_id
    FROM Student_Enrolment se2
    JOIN Degree_Programs dp2 ON se2.degree_program_id = dp2.degree_program_id
    WHERE dp2.degree_summary_name = 'Bachelor'

question: find id of the tv channels that from the countries where have more than two tv channels.

contract_validated.intent: Find IDs of TV channels that are from countries having more than two TV channels.
notes: Use TV_Channel table only, as all needed data (id, Country) is present.
Group by Country to count channels per country.
Filter countries with more than two channels using HAVING.
Select TV_Channel.id to return channel IDs from those countries.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT TV_Channel.id
FROM TV_Channel
WHERE TV_Channel.Country IN (

    FROM TV_Channel
    GROUP BY TV_Channel.Country
    HAVING COUNT(TV_Channel.id) > 2
);

GROUP BY Country
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;

    GROUP BY TV_Channel.Country

question: What are the ids of all tv channels that have more than 2 TV channels?

contract_validated.intent: Find TV channel IDs that appear more than twice in the TV_series or Cartoon tables (i.e., channels with more than 2 associated shows).
notes: Question asks for IDs of TV channels with more than 2 TV channels — logically means channels with more than 2 associated shows.
TV_Channel.id is PK; TV_series.Channel and Cartoon.Channel are FKs to it.
Need to count total shows (TV_series + Cartoon) per channel, so join both tables.
COUNT(*) > 2 in HAVING groups by channel ID.
Planner incorrectly groups by TV_Channel.id and counts TV_Channel.id — this counts channels, not shows.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT TV_Channel.id
FROM TV_Channel
INNER JOIN TV_series ON TV_Channel.id = TV_series.Channel
INNER JOIN Cartoon ON TV_Channel.id = Cartoon.Channel
GROUP BY TV_Channel.id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT id
FROM TV_Channel
GROUP BY id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;

final_sql:
SELECT TV_Channel.id
FROM TV_Channel
INNER JOIN TV_series ON TV_Channel.id = TV_series.Channel
INNER JOIN Cartoon ON TV_Channel.id = Cartoon.Channel
GROUP BY TV_Channel.id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 2;

question: find the id of tv channels that do not play any cartoon directed by Ben Jones.

conditions: Cartoon.Directed_by != 'Ben Jones' OR Cartoon.Directed_by IS NULL

contract_validated.intent: Find TV channel IDs that do not broadcast any cartoon directed by Ben Jones.
notes: Planner incorrectly uses OR IS NULL to exclude Ben Jones cartoons; this does not find channels with NO Ben Jones cartoons.
Correct approach: use NOT IN subquery to exclude channels that broadcast ANY cartoon by Ben Jones.
Cartoon.Directed_by must be compared exactly to 'Ben Jones' (case-sensitive match assumed).
TV_Channel is the only table needed in SELECT; no joins required since subquery handles the filtering.
Planner's plan would return channels with non-Ben Jones cartoons, not channels with zero Ben Jones cartoons.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract: SELECT TV_Channel.id FROM TV_Channel WHERE TV_Channel.id NOT IN (SELECT Cartoon.Channel FROM Cartoon WHERE Cartoon.Directed_by = 'Ben Jones');
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT id FROM TV_Channel
EXCEPT
SELECT DISTINCT c.Channel FROM Cartoon c
WHERE c.Directed_by = 'Ben Jones';

final_sql: SELECT TV_Channel.id FROM TV_Channel WHERE TV_Channel.id NOT IN (SELECT Cartoon.Channel FROM Cartoon WHERE Cartoon.Directed_by = 'Ben Jones');

question: What are the contestant numbers and names of the contestants who had at least two votes?

aggregations: GROUP BY CONTESTANTS.contestant_number, CONTESTANTS.contestant_name HAVING COUNT(VOTES.vote_id) >= 2

notes: Join CONTESTANTS and VOTES on contestant_number to link contestants with their votes.
Group by contestant_number and contestant_name to count votes per contestant.
Use HAVING to filter contestants with at least two votes.
No ordering or limit needed as question asks for all qualifying contestants.
Vote counting uses vote_id as it is the primary key and uniquely identifies each vote.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT CONTESTANTS.contestant_number, CONTESTANTS.contestant_name
FROM CONTESTANTS
INNER JOIN VOTES ON CONTESTANTS.contestant_number = VOTES.contestant_number
GROUP BY CONTESTANTS.contestant_number, CONTESTANTS.contestant_name
HAVING COUNT(VOTES.vote_id) >= 2;

SELECT c.contestant_number, c.contestant_name
FROM CONTESTANTS c
JOIN VOTES v ON c.contestant_number = v.contestant_number
GROUP BY c.contestant_number, c.contestant_name
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 2;

final_sql:

FROM CONTESTANTS
INNER JOIN VOTES ON CONTESTANTS.contestant_number = VOTES.contestant_number
GROUP BY CONTESTANTS.contestant_number, CONTESTANTS.contestant_name
HAVING COUNT(VOTES.vote_id) >= 2;

question: List the area codes in which voters voted both for the contestant 'Tabatha Gehling' and the contestant 'Kelly Clauss'.

contract_validated.intent: Find area codes where voters voted for both Tabatha Gehling and Kelly Clauss.
notes: The planner's WHERE condition incorrectly uses two separate equality checks on contestant_name; this will never match a single row.
Need to use HAVING with COUNT(DISTINCT contestant_name) = 2 to ensure both contestants are voted for in the same area code.
Current plan correctly uses GROUP BY area_code and HAVING, but WHERE must be removed and replaced with filtering via JOIN + HAVING.
The schema allows VOTES.state to join to AREA_CODE_STATE.state; this is valid.
No need for DISTINCT in SELECT since GROUP BY ensures uniqueness per area_code.

reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_contract:
SELECT AREA_CODE_STATE.area_code
FROM AREA_CODE_STATE
INNER JOIN VOTES ON VOTES.state = AREA_CODE_STATE.state
INNER JOIN CONTESTANTS ON VOTES.contestant_number = CONTESTANTS.contestant_number
WHERE CONTESTANTS.contestant_name IN ('Tabatha Gehling', 'Kelly Clauss')
GROUP BY AREA_CODE_STATE.area_code
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT CONTESTANTS.contestant_name) = 2;
reasoner_decision.candidate_sql_oneshot:
SELECT DISTINCT ac1.area_code
FROM AREA_CODE_STATE ac1
JOIN VOTES v1 ON ac1.state = v1.state
JOIN CONTESTANTS c1 ON v1.contestant_number = c1.contestant_number
JOIN AREA_CODE_STATE ac2 ON ac1.area_code = ac2.area_code
JOIN VOTES v2 ON ac2.state = v2.state
JOIN CONTESTANTS c2 ON v2.contestant_number = c2.contestant_number
WHERE c1.contestant_name = 'Tabatha Gehling'
  AND c2.contestant_name = 'Kelly Clauss';

final_sql:
SELECT DISTINCT ac1.area_code
FROM AREA_CODE_STATE ac1
JOIN VOTES v1 ON ac1.state = v1.state
JOIN CONTESTANTS c1 ON v1.contestant_number = c1.contestant_number
JOIN AREA_CODE_STATE ac2 ON ac1.area_code = ac2.area_code
JOIN VOTES v2 ON ac2.state = v2.state
JOIN CONTESTANTS c2 ON v2.contestant_number = c2.contestant_number
WHERE c1.contestant_name = 'Tabatha Gehling'
  AND c2.contestant_name = 'Kelly Clauss';
